deployment_execution_phase.overview: This phase handles the final deployment of the REST connector(s) after all DSL configuration files have been created. It provides four deployment options: Local (Docker), Serverless, Self-hosted Server, and OpenShift.

phase_4_multiple_connectors.title: Multiple Connectors Check
phase_4_multiple_connectors.description: After completing Phase 3 (Presentation & Confirmation) for a connector, ask if the user wants to create additional connectors.

step_1_ask_additional_connectors.title: Ask About Additional Connectors
step_1_ask_additional_connectors.when: After successfully creating and confirming a connector configuration
step_1_ask_additional_connectors.question: Would you like to create another REST API connector configuration?

option.name: yes
option.label: Yes, create another connector
option.action: Return to Phase 1 (API Discovery & Analysis) to create another connector
option.note: All connectors will be stored in the same resources directory

step_1_ask_additional_connectors.presentation_format: Present options clearly: 1. Yes - Create another connector (returns to Phase 1) 2. No - Proceed to deployment

loop_behavior.description: If user chooses to create another connector, repeat Phases 1-3 for the new connector. Continue asking after each connector until user chooses to proceed to deployment.
loop_behavior.note: Multiple connectors can be packaged together in a single deployment. All DSL files in sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources/ will be included in the deployment.

phase_5_deployment_option_selection.title: Deployment Option Selection
phase_5_deployment_option_selection.description: Present four deployment options and guide the user through the selected deployment process.

step_1_present_deployment_options.title: Present Deployment Options
step_1_present_deployment_options.question: How would you like to deploy your REST API connector(s)?
step_1_present_deployment_options.important_note: The deployment options presented depend on the deployment type chosen in Phase 0: - Developer option: Shows all 4 deployment options (Local, Serverless, Self-Hosted, OpenShift) - Codeless option: Shows only 3 deployment options (Serverless, Self-Hosted, OpenShift) Local deployment is not available for codeless option as it requires building custom Docker image.

options_for_developer.title: Options when Developer deployment type was chosen in Phase 0

option.name: local
option.label: Local Docker Deployment
option.description: Build and run Docker container on your local machine. Best for: Testing and manual verification of connectors.

option.name: serverless
option.label: Serverless Deployment
option.description: Deploy Docker container to a serverless platform. Best for: As-a-service solutions with automatic scaling.

option.name: self_hosted
option.label: Self-Hosted Server Deployment
option.description: Deploy Docker container to your own server infrastructure. Best for: As-a-service solutions with full control.

option.name: openshift
option.label: OpenShift Deployment
option.description: Deploy container to OpenShift platform. Best for: On-premises enterprise deployments.

options_for_codeless.title: Options when Codeless deployment type was chosen in Phase 0
options_for_codeless.note: Local Docker deployment is not available - codeless uses pre-built image (ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0)

option.name: serverless
option.label: Serverless Deployment
option.description: Deploy pre-built Docker container to a serverless platform. Best for: As-a-service solutions with automatic scaling.

option.name: self_hosted
option.label: Self-Hosted Server Deployment
option.description: Deploy pre-built Docker container to your own server infrastructure. Best for: As-a-service solutions with full control.

option.name: openshift
option.label: OpenShift Deployment
option.description: Deploy pre-built container to OpenShift platform. Best for: On-premises enterprise deployments.

step_1_present_deployment_options.presentation_format: - For Developer option: Present all four deployment options with clear numbering - For Codeless option: Present only three deployment options (exclude Local Docker) - Renumber options for codeless (1-3 instead of 2-4) - Indicate which options are placeholders (not yet implemented) - Explain that codeless uses pre-built image ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0

sub_step_1_ask_execution_preference.question: Would you like me to execute the deployment commands, or would you prefer to do it yourself?

option.name: auto
option.label: Automatic - Execute commands for me
option.description: I will run all necessary Gradle tasks to build and start the Docker container

option.name: manual
option.label: Manual - I'll do it myself
option.description: I will provide you with step-by-step instructions

action.name: Navigate to sdk-gen
action.note: All Gradle tasks must be run from the sdk-gen directory

action.name: Build Project
action.description: Compiles the project and runs tests
action.note: This may take several minutes on first run

action.name: Generate Flight Application
action.description: Generates the Flight service application with all connectors

action.name: Build Docker Image
action.description: Creates Docker image containing the connector(s)
action.note: Requires Docker to be installed and running

action.name: Start Docker Container
action.description: Starts the Docker container with the connector(s)
action.note: Container will run in the background

action.name: Verify Deployment
action.description: Confirm that the container is running successfully. The connector(s) should now be accessible via the Flight service.

action.name: Inform User
action.message:
Local deployment complete!
Your REST API connector(s) are now running in a Docker container. The Flight service is accessible and ready to use.
To stop the container, run: ./gradlew dockerStop (from sdk-gen directory)

if_build_fails.action: Report the error details to the user
Suggest checking build logs for specific issues
Offer to provide manual instructions instead

if_docker_not_running.action: Inform user that Docker must be installed and running

sub_step_2b_manual_deployment.title: Manual Local Deployment Instructions
sub_step_2b_manual_deployment.instructions:
To deploy your REST API connector(s) locally using Docker, follow these steps:
1. Navigate to the sdk-gen directory: cd sdk-gen
2. Build the project: ./gradlew build
This compiles the project and runs tests. May take several minutes on first run.
3. Generate the Flight application: ./gradlew generateFlightApp
This generates the Flight service application with all your connectors.
4. Build the Docker image: ./gradlew dockerBuild
This creates a Docker image containing your connector(s). Note: Docker must be installed and running on your machine.
5. Start the Docker container: ./gradlew dockerStart
This starts the container with your connector(s) running.
6. Verify the deployment: The connector(s) should now be accessible via the Flight service.
To stop the container later, run: ./gradlew dockerStop
Let me know once you've completed these steps or if you encounter any issues.

wait_for_confirmation.description: Wait for user to confirm they have completed the deployment steps or report any issues encountered.

placeholder_notice.message:
Serverless deployment is not yet implemented.
This is a placeholder for future functionality. When implemented, this option will: - Build the Docker image with your connector(s) - Deploy the image to a serverless platform (e.g., AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Run, Azure Functions) - Configure automatic scaling and endpoint management - Provide the service URL for accessing your connector(s)
For now, please choose one of the other deployment options: - Option 1: Local Docker Deployment (for testing) - Option 3: Self-Hosted Server Deployment (for production)

future_implementation.description: When implemented, this section will include: - Cloud provider selection - Authentication and credentials configuration - Serverless platform-specific deployment commands - Endpoint URL generation and configuration - Monitoring and logging setup

step_4_handle_self_hosted_deployment.title: Self-Hosted Server Deployment
step_4_handle_self_hosted_deployment.important_note: The deployment process differs based on the deployment type chosen in Phase 0: - Codeless: Uses deploy-container.sh script with pre-built image (ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0) - Developer: Uses ./gradlew dockerPush to build and push custom Docker image

sub_step_1_ask_execution_preference.question: Would you like me to execute the deployment, or would you prefer to do it yourself?

option.name: auto
option.label: Automatic - Execute for me
option.description: I will run the deployment commands/script to deploy to your server

option.name: manual
option.label: Manual - I'll do it myself
option.description: I will provide you with instructions

sub_step_2a_codeless_automatic.title: Automatic Self-Hosted Deployment (Codeless)
sub_step_2a_codeless_automatic.when: User chooses automatic execution AND codeless deployment type

action.name: Ask for SSH Connection Details
action.description: Ask the user for SSH connection details using ask_followup_question. Present these as suggested answers with placeholders for the user to fill in.
action.question: Please provide your SSH connection details for the remote Docker server:

suggested_answers.suggest: SSH Host: [hostname/IP], SSH User: [username], SSH Port: 22
SSH Host: [hostname/IP], SSH User: [username], SSH Port: [custom]

action.note: Parse the user's response to extract host, user, and port values.

action.name: Ask About SSH Key Setup
action.description: After receiving SSH connection details, immediately ask if SSH keys are configured.
action.question: Do you have SSH key authentication set up for {ssh-user}@{host}?

suggested_answers.suggest: Yes, I can connect without a password
No, I need help setting up SSH keys

action.name: Provide SSH Setup Instructions (if needed)
action.when: User indicates SSH keys are not set up
action.description: Provide personalized SSH setup commands using the connection details from step 1.

ssh_setup_instructions.title: SSH Key Authentication Setup
ssh_setup_instructions.description: To use the deployment script, you need to set up SSH key authentication. Here are the commands personalized for your server:

for_unix_linux_macos.title: For Unix/Linux/macOS:
for_unix_linux_macos.note: Run this command and enter your password when prompted
for_unix_linux_macos.if_fails: If ssh-copy-id fails or is not available, please consult your system administrator or refer to online SSH documentation for your specific operating system.

for_windows.title: For Windows (PowerShell):
for_windows.note: Run this command and enter your password when prompted
for_windows.if_fails: If this command fails, please consult your system administrator or refer to online SSH documentation for Windows.

verification.title: Verify SSH Access:
verification.success_criteria: You should be able to log in without entering a password

after_providing_instructions.question: Have you completed the SSH key setup? Can you now connect without a password?

suggested_answers.suggest: Yes, SSH keys are now configured
No, I encountered issues - provide manual deployment instructions instead

action.name: Gather Remaining Deployment Parameters
action.when: SSH authentication is confirmed to be working
action.description:
Now ask for the deployment-specific parameters. Host, SSH user, and SSH port were already gathered in step 1.
IMPORTANT: Before asking, list the available connector configuration files from sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources/ and explain which connector each file represents to help non-technical users.

steps.step: For each file, read the "$connector_name" field to identify the connector name
Present the list to the user in a clear format showing: filename → connector name
Ask which files to deploy along with container port and replace flag

action.question_format:
I found the following connector configuration files:
Available connectors: - filename1.json → Connector Name 1 - filename2.json → Connector Name 2
Which configuration files would you like to deploy? Please also specify: - Container port (default: 9090) - Replace existing container? (yes/no, default: no)
action.note: Parse the response to extract: - files list (can be specific filenames or "all") - port (default to 9090 if not specified) - replace flag (default to false if not specified)

action.name: Execute Deployment Script
action.description: Runs the OS-specific deployment script with user-provided parameters. The script handles: - Connecting to remote server via SSH (using your SSH keys) - Pulling the pre-built Docker image (ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0) - Creating and uploading tar archive with configuration files - Deploying the container to the target server - Uploading configuration files to /config/mappings/ in the container - Configuring port mapping (9443 internal → user-specified external) - Starting and verifying the container
action.note: Script uses SSH to deploy to remote server

action.name: Monitor Deployment
action.description: Monitor the script output for successful deployment confirmation. The script provides 7-step progress updates.

action.name: Verify Deployment
action.description: Confirm that the connector(s) are accessible on the target server. The script will output the container ID and name upon success.

action.name: Inform User
action.message:
Self-hosted deployment complete!
Your REST API connector(s) have been deployed to your server using the pre-built image. The deployment script has configured the necessary networking and access.
Container details and service endpoint information are shown in the script output.

if_script_fails.action: Report the error details from script output
Verify SSH key authentication is configured correctly
Check if configuration files exist at specified paths
Verify port number is valid (1-65535) and not in use (unless --replace is used)
Check network connectivity to target server
Verify Docker is installed and user has permissions on remote server
Offer to provide manual instructions instead

sub_step_2b_developer_automatic.title: Automatic Self-Hosted Deployment (Developer)
sub_step_2b_developer_automatic.when: User chooses automatic execution AND developer deployment type

action.name: Navigate to sdk-gen
action.note: Gradle task must be run from sdk-gen directory

action.name: Push Docker Image
action.description: Builds the Docker image with your custom connector(s) and pushes it to the configured container registry. This task handles: - Building the Docker image with your connector code - Tagging the image appropriately - Pushing to your container registry
action.note: Container registry must be configured in gradle.properties or environment variables

action.name: Verify Push
action.description: Confirm that the image was successfully pushed to the container registry.

action.name: Inform User
action.message:
Docker image push complete!
Your custom Docker image with REST API connector(s) has been pushed to the container registry.
Next steps: - Deploy the image to your server using your deployment tools - Configure the service endpoint and networking - Verify the connector(s) are accessible

if_gradle_fails.action: Report the error details from Gradle output
Check if container registry credentials are configured
Check network connectivity to container registry
Offer to provide manual instructions instead

sub_step_2c_codeless_manual.title: Manual Self-Hosted Deployment Instructions (Codeless)
sub_step_2c_codeless_manual.when: User chooses manual execution AND codeless deployment type

ssh_prerequisites.title: SSH Key Authentication Setup (Required)
ssh_prerequisites.description: The deploy-container.sh script uses SSH to connect to your remote Docker server. You MUST set up SSH key authentication before running the deployment script.

setup_for_unix_linux_macos.title: Unix/Linux/macOS Setup
setup_for_unix_linux_macos.note: Replace 'user' with your username and 'remote-host' with your server address
setup_for_unix_linux_macos.if_fails: If ssh-copy-id fails or is not available, please consult your system administrator or refer to online SSH documentation for your specific operating system.

setup_for_windows.title: Windows Setup
setup_for_windows.note: Replace 'user' with your username and 'remote-host' with your server address
setup_for_windows.if_fails: If this command fails, please consult your system administrator or refer to online SSH documentation for Windows.

verification.title: Verify SSH Access
verification.success_criteria: You should be able to log in without entering a password

sub_step_2c_codeless_manual.instructions:
To deploy your REST API connector(s) to your self-hosted server using the pre-built image:
IMPORTANT: Before proceeding, ensure SSH key authentication is configured (see SSH Prerequisites above).
REQUIRED PARAMETERS: You need to provide the following information to the deployment script:
1. --host: Remote server hostname or IP address (e.g., remote-server.com or 192.168.1.100) SSH must be accessible on this host.
2. --ssh-user: SSH username for remote server (e.g., ubuntu) User must have Docker permissions on remote server.
3. --files: Space-separated list of connector configuration JSON files Location: sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources/ Example: api1.json api2.json
4. --port: Port to expose on the host machine (1-65535) This will be mapped to the container's internal port 9443. Example: 9090
OPTIONAL PARAMETERS: 5. --ssh-port: SSH port (default: 22) Example: 2222
6. --replace: Add this flag to stop and remove existing container using the same name or port
For Unix/Linux/macOS: 1. Ensure the deployment script has execute permissions: chmod +x connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-container.sh
2. Run the deployment script with required parameters: ./connectors-forge/codeless/deploy-container.sh \ --host remote-server.com \ --ssh-user ubuntu \ --files sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources/api1.json api2.json \ --port 9090 \ [--ssh-port 22] \ [--replace]
For Windows: 1. Run the PowerShell deployment script with required parameters: .\connectors-forge\codeless\deploy-container.ps1 ` -Host "remote-server.com" ` -SshUser "ubuntu" ` -Files "sdk-gen\subprojects\connectors_forge_rest\src\main\resources\api1.json","api2.json" ` -Port 9090 ` [-SshPort 22] ` [-Replace]
The script will: - Connect to your remote server via SSH (using your SSH keys) - Pull the pre-built Docker image (ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0) - Create and upload tar archive with your configuration files - Deploy the container to your target server - Upload configuration files to /config/mappings/ in the container - Configure port mapping (9443 internal → your specified external port) - Start and verify the container
Prerequisites: - SSH key authentication configured (REQUIRED - see SSH Prerequisites above) - Docker installed on the remote server - User has Docker permissions on remote server - Network connectivity to the target server - Configuration files exist at the specified paths - Windows: PowerShell 5.1 or later
The script will output the container ID, name, and deployment status upon successful completion.
Let me know once you've completed the deployment or if you encounter any issues.

wait_for_confirmation.description: Wait for user to confirm they have completed the deployment or report any issues encountered.

sub_step_2d_developer_manual.title: Manual Self-Hosted Deployment Instructions (Developer)
sub_step_2d_developer_manual.when: User chooses manual execution AND developer deployment type
sub_step_2d_developer_manual.instructions:
To deploy your custom REST API connector(s) to your self-hosted server:
1. Navigate to the sdk-gen directory: cd sdk-gen
2. Build and push your Docker image to the container registry: ./gradlew dockerPush
This will: - Build a Docker image with your custom connector code - Tag the image appropriately - Push it to your configured container registry
Prerequisites: - Docker installed and running on your machine - Container registry credentials configured in gradle.properties or environment variables - Network connectivity to the container registry
3. After the image is pushed, deploy it to your server using your deployment tools
4. Configure the service endpoint and networking on your server
5. Verify the connector(s) are accessible
Let me know once you've completed the deployment or if you encounter any issues.

wait_for_confirmation.description: Wait for user to confirm they have completed the deployment or report any issues encountered.

step_5_handle_openshift_deployment.title: OpenShift Deployment
step_5_handle_openshift_deployment.when: User selects option 3 (OpenShift) for codeless deployment
step_5_handle_openshift_deployment.important_note: This step is for users who want to deploy their connector(s) to OpenShift. It uses the pre-built Docker image (ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0) from GitHub Container Registry.

sub_step_1_ask_execution_preference.question: Would you like me to execute the OpenShift deployment commands, or would you prefer to do it yourself?

option.name: auto
option.label: Automatic - Execute commands for me
option.description: I will run the OpenShift CLI commands to deploy your connector(s)

option.name: manual
option.label: Manual - I'll do it myself
option.description: I will provide you with step-by-step instructions

prerequisites.requirement: User must have OpenShift cluster credentials
User must have permissions to create resources in the target project

action.name: Gather OpenShift Connection Details
action.description: Ask the user for OpenShift connection details: 1. OpenShift cluster URI (e.g., https://api.cluster.example.com:6443) 2. Username 3. Password 4. Project/namespace name (where to deploy the connector)

action.name: Login to OpenShift
action.description: Authenticate with the OpenShift cluster using provided credentials.

action.name: Select Project
action.description: Switch to the target project/namespace where the connector will be deployed.

action.name: Create ConfigMap from Connector Files
action.description: Create a ConfigMap containing all connector configuration JSON files. This ConfigMap will be mounted into the container at /config/mappings.
action.note: If configmap already exists, delete it first: oc delete configmap connectors-config

action.name: Apply Deployment Configuration
action.description: Deploy the connector container using the deployment configuration. This creates a Deployment resource with the connector image and mounts the ConfigMap.

action.name: Apply Service Configuration
action.description: Create a Service to expose the connector within the cluster. The service exposes port 9443 for gRPC communication.

action.name: Verify Deployment
action.description: Check that the connector pod is running successfully.

action.name: Get Service Details
action.description: Display the service details including the ClusterIP and port.

action.name: Inform User
action.message:
OpenShift deployment complete!
Your REST API connector(s) have been deployed to OpenShift.
Deployment details: - Namespace: {project_name} - Deployment: cf-flight-connector - Service: cf-flight-connector-service - Port: 9443 (gRPC)
The connector is accessible within the cluster at: cf-flight-connector-service.{project_name}.svc.cluster.local:9443

if_login_fails.action: Verify OpenShift cluster URI is correct
Check username and password are valid
Ensure network connectivity to the cluster

if_project_not_found.action: Check user has access to the project
Offer to create the project if it doesn't exist

if_configmap_creation_fails.action: Check if configmap already exists (delete and recreate)
Verify connector configuration files exist in the specified path

if_deployment_fails.action: Check pod logs: oc logs -l app=cf-flight-connector
Verify image can be pulled from GitHub Container Registry
Check resource quotas in the namespace

sub_step_2b_manual_deployment.title: Manual OpenShift Deployment Instructions
sub_step_2b_manual_deployment.instructions:
To deploy your REST API connector(s) to OpenShift:
PREREQUISITES: - OpenShift CLI (oc) installed on your machine - Access to an OpenShift cluster - Permissions to create resources in your target project/namespace
DEPLOYMENT STEPS:
1. Login to your OpenShift cluster: oc login -u -p
Example: oc login -u developer -p mypassword https://api.cluster.example.com:6443
2. Select the project (namespace) where you want to deploy: oc project
Example: oc project connectorsforge
If the project doesn't exist, create it first: oc new-project connectorsforge
3. Create a ConfigMap with your connector configuration files: oc create configmap connectors-config --from-file=sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources
This command: - Creates a ConfigMap named "connectors-config" - Includes all JSON files from the specified directory - These files will be mounted in the container at /config/mappings
If the ConfigMap already exists, delete it first: oc delete configmap connectors-config
4. Deploy the connector using the deployment configuration: oc apply -f connectors-forge/codeless/deployment.yaml
This creates: - A Deployment with 1 replica - Uses image: ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0 - Mounts the connectors-config ConfigMap - Exposes port 9443 for gRPC
5. Create the service to expose the connector: oc apply -f connectors-forge/codeless/service.yaml
This creates: - A ClusterIP service - Exposes port 9443 - Makes the connector accessible within the cluster
6. Verify the deployment: oc get pods -l app=cf-flight-connector
Wait until the pod status shows "Running"
7. Check the service: oc get service cf-flight-connector-service
Note the ClusterIP and port for accessing the connector
8. View logs (if needed): oc logs -l app=cf-flight-connector
This helps troubleshoot any issues
ACCESSING THE CONNECTOR: The connector is accessible within the OpenShift cluster at: cf-flight-connector-service..svc.cluster.local:9443
For external access, you may need to create a Route: oc expose service cf-flight-connector-service
UPDATING THE DEPLOYMENT: If you need to update connector configurations: 1. Delete the existing ConfigMap: oc delete configmap connectors-config 2. Recreate it with updated files: oc create configmap connectors-config --from-file=sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_rest/src/main/resources 3. Restart the deployment: oc rollout restart deployment cf-flight-connector
Let me know once you've completed the deployment or if you encounter any issues.

wait_for_confirmation.description: Wait for user to confirm they have completed the deployment or report any issues encountered.

deployment_yaml.description: Defines the Kubernetes Deployment resource: - Image: ghcr.io/marek-zuwala/connectors-forge:1.0.0 - Namespace: connectorsforge (can be changed) - ConfigMap mount: /config/mappings - Resource limits: 256Mi memory, 200m CPU

service_yaml.description: Defines the Kubernetes Service resource: - Type: ClusterIP - Port: 9443 (gRPC) - Selector: app=cf-flight-connector

phase_6_cp4d_registration.title: Cloud Pak for Data Registration
phase_6_cp4d_registration.description: After successful deployment, register the connector(s) with Cloud Pak for Data. The registration process differs based on the deployment type chosen in Phase 0.

step_1_ask_registration_preference.title: Ask Registration Preference
step_1_ask_registration_preference.question: Would you like to register your connector(s) with Cloud Pak for Data now?

option.name: yes
option.label: Yes, register now
option.action: Proceed to registration process based on deployment type

option.name: no
option.label: No, I'll register later
option.action: Skip registration and complete the workflow

step_1_ask_registration_preference.presentation_format: Present options clearly: 1. Yes - Register connector(s) with CP4D now 2. No - Skip registration (can be done later)

step_2_developer_registration.title: Developer Option Registration
step_2_developer_registration.when: User chooses registration AND developer deployment type was selected in Phase 0

sub_step_1_verify_properties_file.title: Verify Properties File Creation
sub_step_1_verify_properties_file.important_note: Before registration, you need to create a properties file with your CP4D environment configuration. This file is not visible to me (it's in .bobignore), so you need to create it manually.

step.name: Create Properties File
step.description:
Create a properties file in the following location: sdk-gen/subprojects/flight/src/dist/resources/payload/envs/
Use a descriptive name like: myenv.properties, production.properties, or dev.properties
Base your file on the template at: sdk-gen/subprojects/flight/src/dist/resources/payload/envs/template.properties

step.name: Configure Required Properties
step.description: Your properties file must include: - apikey: Your IBM Cloud API key - auth_uri: Authentication endpoint (default: https://iam.cloud.ibm.com/identity/token) - datasource_types_uri: Datasource types API endpoint - flight_uri: URI of your deployed Flight service (grpc://host:port) - ssl_certificate_path: Path to your Flight service certificate (optional) - ssl_certificate_validation: Set to true/false

sub_step_1_verify_properties_file.confirmation_question: Have you created the properties file with your CP4D environment configuration? Please provide the name of your properties file (without .properties extension).

sub_step_2_ask_execution_preference.title: Ask Execution Preference
sub_step_2_ask_execution_preference.when: After user confirms properties file is created
sub_step_2_ask_execution_preference.question: Would you like me to execute the registration, or would you prefer to do it yourself?

option.name: auto
option.label: Automatic - Execute registration for me
option.description: I will run the Gradle register task with your environment configuration

option.name: manual
option.label: Manual - I'll do it myself
option.description: I will provide you with instructions to run the registration

action.name: Navigate to sdk-gen
action.note: Gradle task must be run from sdk-gen directory

action.name: Run Registration Task
action.description: Registers the connector(s) with Cloud Pak for Data using the specified environment configuration. Replace {env_name} with the properties file name provided by the user.
action.note: The env_name should match the properties file name without .properties extension

action.name: Verify Registration
action.description: Confirm that the registration completed successfully by checking the task output.

action.name: Inform User
action.message:
Registration complete!
Your REST API connector(s) have been registered with Cloud Pak for Data. The connector(s) are now available for use in your CP4D environment.
You can now create connections and access your REST API data through CP4D.

if_registration_fails.action: Report the error details from Gradle output
Verify the properties file exists and contains correct values
Check network connectivity to CP4D endpoints
Verify API key has proper permissions
Offer to provide manual instructions instead

sub_step_3b_manual_registration.title: Manual Registration Instructions (Developer)
sub_step_3b_manual_registration.instructions:
To register your REST API connector(s) with Cloud Pak for Data:
1. Ensure you have created your properties file in: sdk-gen/subprojects/flight/src/dist/resources/payload/envs/{your_env_name}.properties
2. Navigate to the sdk-gen directory: cd sdk-gen
3. Run the registration task with your environment name: ./gradlew register -Penv={your_env_name}
Replace {your_env_name} with your properties file name (without .properties extension) For example, if your file is myenv.properties, use: -Penv=myenv
The registration task will: - Authenticate with IBM Cloud using your API key - Register the connector datasource type with CP4D - Configure the Flight service endpoint - Make the connector(s) available in your CP4D environment
Prerequisites: - Valid IBM Cloud API key with proper permissions - Network connectivity to CP4D endpoints - Deployed and accessible Flight service
Let me know once you've completed the registration or if you encounter any issues.

wait_for_confirmation.description: Wait for user to confirm they have completed the registration or report any issues encountered.

step_3_codeless_registration.title: Codeless Option Registration
step_3_codeless_registration.when: User chooses registration AND codeless deployment type was selected in Phase 0

sub_step_1_verify_properties_file.title: Create Properties File from Template
sub_step_1_verify_properties_file.important_note: Before registration, you need to create a properties file based on the provided template. This file is not visible to me (it's in .bobignore), so you need to create it manually.

step.name: Review Template File
step.description:
A template file is provided at: connectors-forge/codeless/register-envs.properties.template
This template shows the required configuration structure with placeholder values.

step.name: Create Properties File
step.description:
Copy the template file to create your configuration:
From: connectors-forge/codeless/register-envs.properties.template To: connectors-forge/codeless/register-envs.properties
You can do this with: - Unix/Linux/macOS: cp connectors-forge/codeless/register-envs.properties.template connectors-forge/codeless/register-envs.properties - Windows: copy connectors-forge\codeless\register-envs.properties.template connectors-forge\codeless\register-envs.properties

step.name: Configure Required Properties
step.description:
Edit register-envs.properties and replace all placeholder values (marked with ):
REQUIRED properties to configure: - apikey: Replace with your IBM Cloud API key - flight_hostname: Replace with your deployed Flight service hostname - flight_port: Replace with your Flight service port number
OPTIONAL properties (defaults provided): - auth_uri: Authentication endpoint (default: https://iam.cloud.ibm.com/identity/token) - datasource_types_uri: Datasource types API endpoint (default provided in template) - ssl_certificate_path: Path to SSL certificate (commented out by default) - ssl_certificate_validation: Enable/disable SSL validation (default: false)
step.example: Example of configured values: apikey=your_actual_ibm_cloud_api_key_here auth_uri=https://iam.cloud.ibm.com/identity/token datasource_types_uri=https://api.dataplatform.cloud.ibm.com:443/v2/datasource_types flight_hostname=my-server.example.com flight_port=50051 ssl_certificate_validation=false

sub_step_1_verify_properties_file.confirmation_question: Have you created register-envs.properties from the template and configured all required values (apikey, flight_hostname, flight_port)?

sub_step_2_ask_execution_preference.title: Ask Execution Preference
sub_step_2_ask_execution_preference.when: After user confirms properties file is configured
sub_step_2_ask_execution_preference.question: Would you like me to execute the registration, or would you prefer to do it yourself?

option.name: auto
option.label: Automatic - Execute registration for me
option.description: I will run the registration script with your configuration

option.name: manual
option.label: Manual - I'll do it myself
option.description: I will provide you with instructions to run the script

action.name: Execute Registration Script
action.description: Runs the OS-specific registration script that handles: - Reading configuration from register-envs.properties - Authenticating with IBM Cloud - Registering the connector with CP4D - Configuring the Flight service endpoint
action.note: The script selection is based on the operating system: - Unix/Linux/macOS: register-connector.sh (requires execute permissions: chmod +x) - Windows: register-connector.ps1 (PowerShell script)

action.name: Monitor Registration
action.description: Monitor the script output for successful registration confirmation.

action.name: Verify Registration
action.description: Confirm that the connector(s) are registered and available in CP4D.

action.name: Inform User
action.message:
Registration complete!
Your REST API connector(s) have been registered with Cloud Pak for Data. The connector(s) are now available for use in your CP4D environment.
You can now create connections and access your REST API data through CP4D.

if_script_fails.action: Report the error details from script output
Verify the properties file is configured correctly
Check network connectivity to CP4D endpoints
Verify API key has proper permissions
Offer to provide manual instructions instead

sub_step_3b_manual_registration.title: Manual Registration Instructions (Codeless)
sub_step_3b_manual_registration.instructions:
To register your REST API connector(s) with Cloud Pak for Data:
1. Ensure you have configured the properties file at: connectors-forge/codeless/register-envs.properties
For Unix/Linux/macOS: 2. Ensure the script has execute permissions: chmod +x connectors-forge/codeless/register-connector.sh
3. Run the registration script: connectors-forge/codeless/register-connector.sh
For Windows: 2. Run the PowerShell registration script: .\connectors-forge\codeless\register-connector.ps1
The registration script will: - Read your configuration from register-envs.properties - Authenticate with IBM Cloud using your API key - Register the connector datasource type with CP4D - Configure the Flight service endpoint - Make the connector(s) available in your CP4D environment
Prerequisites: - Valid IBM Cloud API key with proper permissions - Network connectivity to CP4D endpoints - Deployed and accessible Flight service - Properly configured register-envs.properties file - Windows: PowerShell 5.1 or later
Let me know once you've completed the registration or if you encounter any issues.

wait_for_confirmation.description: Wait for user to confirm they have completed the registration or report any issues encountered.

phase_7_registration_validation.title: Registration Validation & Testing
phase_7_registration_validation.description: After successful registration, validate that the connector(s) are properly registered and accessible in Cloud Pak for Data by running discovery tests.
phase_7_registration_validation.when: After Phase 6 registration is completed (either automatic or manual)

step_1_ask_validation_preference.title: Ask About Validation Testing
step_1_ask_validation_preference.question:
Would you like to validate the registration by testing connector discovery?
This will verify that: - The connector(s) are properly registered in CP4D - The Flight service is accessible - Discovery queries return expected results

option.name: yes
option.label: Yes, validate the registration
option.description: I will guide you through creating a test configuration and running validation tests

option.name: no
option.label: No, skip validation
option.description: Skip validation testing and complete the workflow

step_1_ask_validation_preference.presentation_format: Present options clearly: 1. Yes - Validate registration with discovery tests 2. No - Skip validation (can be done later)

step_2_prepare_test_configuration.title: Prepare Test Configuration
step_2_prepare_test_configuration.important_note: The test configuration file (test-discovery.properties) is not visible to me as it's in .bobignore. I will suggest the content based on the connector information from this session, but you need to create the file manually with your actual credentials.

sub_step_1_suggest_configuration.title: Suggest Test Configuration Content
sub_step_1_suggest_configuration.action:
Based on the connector(s) created in this session, suggest the content for test-discovery.properties file.
The suggestion should include: 1. Template location reference 2. CPD_URL placeholder (user must provide their CP4D instance URL) 3. CPD_TOKEN placeholder (user must provide their bearer token) 4. DATASOURCE_TYPES - comma-separated list of datasource type IDs from the connector descriptors 5. DISCOVERY_PATHS - comma-separated list of discovery paths (first endpoint path from each connector) 6. CONNECTION_PROPERTIES - pipe-separated JSON objects with connection properties for each connector
For each connector, extract: - Datasource type ID from the connector descriptor ($connector_name field, converted to lowercase with underscores) - First endpoint path from the $tables configuration - Authentication type and required properties from $authentication field - Base URL from $hostname field
sub_step_1_suggest_configuration.example_suggestion_format:
Based on your connector configuration, here's the suggested content for test-discovery.properties:
```properties # Copy from connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.properties.template # and fill in your actual values
# CPD Configuration (YOU MUST PROVIDE THESE) CPD_URL=https://your-cpd-instance.cloud.ibm.com CPD_TOKEN=your-bearer-token-here
# Connector Configuration (based on your connectors) DATASOURCE_TYPES=connector1_id,connector2_id DISCOVERY_PATHS=/endpoint1,/endpoint2 CONNECTION_PROPERTIES={"base_url":"https://api1.example.com","api_key":"YOUR_KEY"}|{"base_url":"https://api2.example.com"} ```
Replace: - CPD_URL with your Cloud Pak for Data instance URL - CPD_TOKEN with your authentication token - YOUR_KEY and other credentials with actual values
sub_step_1_suggest_configuration.instructions:
1. Create a file named test-discovery.properties in the connectors-forge/codeless/ directory
2. Use the template at connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.properties.template as a reference
3. Copy the suggested configuration above and replace placeholders with your actual values: - CPD_URL: Your Cloud Pak for Data instance URL - CPD_TOKEN: Your bearer token (get from CP4D UI or ibmcloud CLI) - API keys, passwords, and other credentials in CONNECTION_PROPERTIES
4. Ensure the file is saved in the correct location: connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.properties
sub_step_1_suggest_configuration.confirmation_question: Have you created the test-discovery.properties file with your configuration?

step_3_execute_validation_tests.title: Execute Validation Tests
step_3_execute_validation_tests.when: User confirms test configuration file is created

action.name: Execute Test Script
action.description: Runs the OS-specific test script that: - Reads configuration from test-discovery.properties - Calls CP4D Connection Service API for each connector - Tests discovery without creating saved connections - Reports success/failure for each connector - Counts discovered assets
action.note: The script selection is based on the operating system: - Unix/Linux/macOS: test-discovery.sh (requires execute permissions: chmod +x) - Windows: test-discovery.ps1 (PowerShell script)

action.name: Monitor Test Results
action.description: Monitor the script output for: - HTTP status codes (200-299 = success) - Number of assets discovered per connector - Any error messages or failures

action.name: Analyze Results
action.description: The script will provide: - Summary of successful vs failed tests - Detailed results for each connector - Asset counts for successful discoveries - Error messages for failures

success_criteria.criterion: All connectors return HTTP 200-299 status codes
Discovery returns expected assets for each connector

if_tests_fail.action: Review error messages from script output
Verify CPD_URL and CPD_TOKEN are correct
Check that DATASOURCE_TYPES match registered connector IDs
Ensure Flight service is deployed and accessible
Check network connectivity to CP4D and Flight service
Offer to re-run tests after fixes

success_message.message:
✓ Registration validation successful!
All connector(s) are properly registered and accessible in Cloud Pak for Data. Discovery tests completed successfully with assets returned for each connector.
Your connectors are ready to use in CP4D.

partial_success_message.message:
⚠ Partial validation success
Some connectors passed validation while others failed. Review the detailed results above to identify issues with specific connectors.
Successful connectors are ready to use. Failed connectors need troubleshooting before they can be used.

failure_message.message:
✗ Registration validation failed
None of the connectors passed validation tests. This typically indicates: - Registration did not complete successfully - Flight service is not accessible - Configuration errors in test-discovery.properties - Network connectivity issues
Please review the error messages and troubleshooting tips above.

manual_validation_option.title: Manual Validation Instructions
manual_validation_option.when: User prefers to run validation manually
manual_validation_option.instructions:
To manually validate your connector registration:
1. Create test-discovery.properties file: - Location: connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.properties - Template: connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.properties.template - Fill in your CPD_URL, CPD_TOKEN, and connector configurations
2. Run the test script:
For Unix/Linux/macOS: chmod +x connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.sh connectors-forge/codeless/test-discovery.sh
For Windows: .\connectors-forge\codeless\test-discovery.ps1
3. Review the test results: - Green checkmarks (✓) indicate successful discovery - Red X marks (✗) indicate failures - The script will show asset counts and error messages
4. Troubleshoot any failures using the tips provided by the script
Let me know the results or if you need help interpreting them.

completion.title: Workflow Complete
completion.description: After successful deployment, optional registration, and optional validation, provide summary and next steps.

include.item: How to access and test the connector(s)

next_steps.step: Test the connector(s) by making API calls through the Flight service
If registered with CP4D: Create connections in CP4D UI
Configure client applications to use the connector endpoint

behavioral_rules.rule: Always ask about additional connectors after completing each configuration
Present all four deployment options clearly, indicating placeholders
For implemented options (Local, Self-Hosted), offer automatic or manual execution
For placeholder options (Serverless, OpenShift), explain future functionality
Execute Gradle tasks sequentially, waiting for each to complete
All Gradle tasks must be run from the sdk-gen directory
Provide clear error messages and recovery options

important_notes.note: Multiple connectors can be created and deployed together in a single container. All DSL files in the resources directory will be included.
Local deployment requires Docker to be installed and running on the user's machine.
Self-hosted deployment requires proper server credentials and network access.
Serverless and OpenShift deployments are placeholders for future implementation.
The deployment script (deploy-container.sh) must have execute permissions.
